does: This paper introduces Alignment-Enhanced Decoding (AED), a token-level defense mechanism that operates during language model inference to prevent jailbreak attacks. The method defines a Competitive Index metric to quantify alignment failures by measuring conflicts between harmful and harmless objectives in the model's predictions. AED uses self-evaluation feedback to compute post-alignment logits, then adaptively combines these with original logits to shift probability distributions toward safer outputs while preserving helpful generation. The approach is evaluated across five different language models and four common jailbreak attack types, demonstrating improved safety without significant degradation of utility.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research addressing safety vulnerabilities in large language models, extending beyond prior defenses that focus on input perturbation or inspection methods. It responds to the recognition that jailbreak failures stem from fundamental misalignment between competing objectives rather than merely surface-level input patterns. The paper connects to work on decoding-time intervention strategies and self-evaluation techniques that have shown promise for safety enhancement. It also relates to broader literature on understanding and mitigating conflicts between model objectives, drawing on methods that use feedback signals to guide model behavior toward safer distributions during generation.
enables: This work opens pathways for developing more sophisticated adaptive decoding strategies that address root causes of misalignment. Future research could extend the Competitive Index framework to other safety objectives or adversarial scenarios beyond jailbreaks. The self-evaluation feedback mechanism could be enhanced through better calibration techniques or integration with external reward models. The approach suggests promise for combining decoding-time defenses with training-time alignment improvements, potentially enabling more robust multi-layer safety systems. Additionally, this method could inspire similar techniques for other challenging decoding problems where competing objectives arise, from multi-objective optimization in generation to constraint satisfaction during inference.
